title: Chapter 2: The Welcome Packet
content: Chloe woke to the smell of decaf coffee and someone crying in the next room.
She sat up. The ward was already bright, beige curtains doing nothing to hold back the morning. Tiffany was at the window, hugging her knees. Mia's bed was empty — tablet gone, sheets still warm.
A laminated card sat on Chloe's bedside table.

**Serenity Peak Wellness Center**
*Daily Schedule - Tier: Red*

06:00 - Wake and Vital Check
06:30 - Group Meditation
07:30 - Breakfast (mandatory attendance)
09:00 - Therapeutic Session A
11:00 - Recreational Period (structured)
12:30 - Lunch
14:00 - Therapeutic Session B
16:00 - Quiet Reflection
17:30 - Dinner
19:00 - Group Processing
21:00 - Lights Out
Chloe read it twice. Then she laughed.
"What?" Tiffany whispered.
"They scheduled every second of the day. Even 'Quiet Reflection.'" She held up the card. "This isn't a hospital. It's Google Calendar with worse coffee."
Breakfast was oatmeal. Not bad oatmeal — brown sugar and dried fruit, served in real ceramic bowls. The kind that looked like care.
Chloe sat next to Tiffany. Mia joined them a minute later, sliding into her seat with the fluid silence of someone who'd learned to move without being noticed.

"I found the camera blind spots," Mia said, stirring her oatmeal. "The dining hall has four. One behind the server station, one by the emergency exit, two in the corners. Gap between table six and the window."
"You mapped the cameras before breakfast?" Tiffany's eyes went wide.
"I was bored."
Chloe smiled. "That's the most dangerous thing you could be in a place like this."
Therapeutic Session A was in a room called the Solarium. It had windows — actual windows, not reinforced — overlooking a manicured garden. The irony wasn't lost on Chloe.
Eight patients sat in a circle. Nurse Cross presided, her cardigan a soft cream that made her look like someone's grandmother.
"Let's start with introductions," Nurse Cross said. "Share your name and why you're here."
The woman to Chloe's left went first. Mid-forties, hollow-eyed. "I'm Denise. I'm here because I can't stop checking my email."
"Work-life balance challenges," Nurse Cross translated smoothly. "Thank you, Denise."
The next woman. "Sarah. I'm here because I told my husband I wanted a divorce."
"Relationship reconciliation," Nurse Cross said.
Chloe felt something hot rise in her chest. Every rebellion, translated into pathology.
When it was her turn, she leaned forward.

"I'm Chloe. I'm here because I refused to work eighty-hour weeks for my father's company, broke up with a man who thought 'no' was optional, and declined a nose job my mother booked for my twenty-second birthday."
Silence.
Nurse Cross's smile didn't waver. "Thank you for sharing, Chloe. It sounds like you're carrying a lot of anger."
"Anger? No. That's not anger. That's just me saying what happened. Maybe the anger is in how you translate it."
The woman next to Chloe — Sarah, the one who wanted a divorce — let out a small, surprised laugh.
Nurse Cross's eyes flickered. Just briefly. But Chloe saw it.
*Good. A crack.*

Recreational Period was a joke — thirty minutes in a courtyard with a cracked birdbath and a fence that was very clearly electrified. But it had one thing the ward did not: a spot where the wifi signal from the admin building leaked through.
Chloe found Mia sitting against the far wall, tablet balanced on her knees, face lit with concentration.
"You found a signal?"
"Weak. But it is there." Mia did not look up. "Admin building runs a separate network. Default SSID, default password. PeakAdmin / admin1234."
"That is their password?"
"Embarrassing, right? I have already mapped their directory structure."
Chloe sat down next to her. The grass was damp. "What did you find?"

"Patient files. Financial records. A folder called 'Special Projects.'" Mia's fingers moved. "The patient files are fascinating. None of us have actual diagnoses. Just referral notes from family members."
"Show me."
Mia turned the tablet. Chloe read:

*Chloe Hart. Referral source: Richard Hart (father), Jake Hart (ex-partner). Presenting concerns: refusal to cooperate with workplace expectations, romantic rejection of suitable partner, elective cosmetic surgery refusal. Recommended program: Tier 1 Adjustment, 8-12 weeks.*
"'Suitable partner,'" Chloe read aloud. "They put 'suitable partner' in a medical file. Like Jake was a prescription I didn't fill."
"Your file's the longest. Want to see mine?"

Mia's file was one line: *Mia Chen. Referral source: parents. Presenting concerns: excessive screen time, argumentative behavior, suspicion of corporate data theft. Recommended program: Tier 2 Adjustment, 12-16 weeks.*
"Your parents accused you of data theft?"
"Because I found it. My father's company was selling user location data without consent. I told him. He told my mother. Two weeks later I was here."
Chloe looked at the fence, at the electrified wires, at the cameras on every corner of the building.
"Mia. Do you trust me?"
Mia looked at her for a long moment. Then she nodded.
"Good. Because I'm going to need your help burning this place down."
That night, Chloe couldn't sleep.
She lay in bed and watched the moonlight crawl across the ceiling. Somewhere, she could hear the ventilation system humming. Somewhere, cameras were recording.
She thought about her mother's face at the gate. The way she'd adjusted her sunglasses. The way she hadn't looked back when the car drove away.
She thought about Jake. His reasonable voice. His calm explanations. 'This is for your own good, Chloe.'
She thought about the woman in group therapy who'd laughed when Chloe spoke. Sarah. The one who'd said she wanted a divorce.
Sarah had been moved to a different ward after lunch. Chloe hadn't seen her since.
Coincidence? Probably not.
Chloe reached under her pillow and touched the edge of the laminated welcome packet. The schedule. The rules. The tier system.
She'd memorized it. Every rule had a workaround. Every system had a blind spot.
And Mia had the passwords.
Tomorrow, she was going to start smuggling.
But tonight, she needed to survive lights-out.
The buzzer sounded at 20:58. Two minutes to curfew. Chloe lay back on her bed and stared at the ceiling. The same ceiling. The same beige. The same hum.
Through the wall, she heard Tiffany's voice — barely a whisper. "Chloe?"
"Yeah."
"Thank you. For the quinoa thing. Nobody's ever said that."
"Said what?"
"That I had a choice."
Chloe didn't know what to say to that. So she just said: "You do. Always."
She heard Tiffany's breathing even out into sleep.
And Chloe smiled in the darkness.
She had no phone. No laptop. No way out.
But she had an ally who could crack any server. A friend who was learning to say no. And a plan that was still taking shape in her head.
It was enough.
For now.

The ward settled into its nightly rhythm — the ventilation hum, the distant buzz of a fluorescent light, the soft creak of an orderly's footsteps in the hallway. Somewhere, a camera was watching. Somewhere, Nurse Cross was probably reviewing the day's footage.
Let her watch. Tomorrow, Chloe would give her something worth reviewing.
She closed her eyes and let herself drift. For the first time in months, she wasn't scared of what tomorrow would bring. She was curious.
**End of Chapter 2**
